Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: We are given an event log table event_log with columns case_id activity timestamp Steps 1 For each case we need to get the ordered sequence of activities by timestamp

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: We can use window functions to assign a row number per case by timestamp then aggregate the activities in that order

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: In DuckDB we can use list activity ORDER BY timestamp to get an ordered list of activities per case

Name: Validation - IND
Text: However note We are going to group by this list But DuckDB does support grouping by a list Actually we can use list as an aggregate and then group by the list

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Step 1 Get the ordered sequence per case We can do SELECT case_id list activity ORDER BY timestamp AS activity_sequence FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Step 2 Now we have for each case the activity sequence We can then group by this sequence to get the variant counts SELECT activity_sequence COUNT AS variant_count FROM case_sequences GROUP BY activity_sequence ORDER BY variant_count DESC

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Step 3 Then we want the top K variants We can use LIMIT K or FETCH FIRST K ROWS ONLY

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Step 4 Now we want to return all events from the original event log for the cases that are in these top K variants

Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: However note The problem says we are to return all events for the cases that are in the top K variants meaning we do not just return the variant sequence but every event of those cases

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: We can do WITH case_sequences AS SELECT case_id list activity ORDER BY timestamp AS activity_sequence FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id variant_counts AS SELECT activity_sequence COUNT AS variant_count FROM case_sequences GROUP BY activity_sequence ORDER BY variant_count DESC LIMIT K SELECT el FROM event_log el JOIN case_sequences cs ON el case_id equals cs case_id JOIN variant_counts vc ON cs activity_sequence equals vc activity_sequence

Name: Counterfactual Reasoning - IND
Text: But what if there are ties The problem does not specify how to handle ties

Name: Abductive Reasoning - IND
Text: We assume we want exactly K so we use LIMIT K

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Alternatively we can use RANK over order by COUNT descending as variant_rank and filter with variant_rank less than or equal to K

Name: Counterfactual Reasoning - IND
Text: This handles ties by including all variants that are in the top K ranks But note the problem says top K variants by their frequency which might mean we break ties arbitrarily to get exactly K

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: Since the problem does not specify I will use RANK and then filter by variant_rank less than or equal to K

Name: Validation - IND
Text: I think using RANK is more appropriate because we are grouping by frequency

Name: Counterfactual Reasoning - IND
Text: Alternatively if we want exactly K we can use ROW_NUMBER to break ties arbitrarily

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: However the problem does not specify so I will use RANK because it is more inclusive and does not arbitrarily break ties

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Alternatively we can use FETCH FIRST K ROWS WITH TIES in the variant_counts CTE

Name: Validation - PE
Text: This will return the top K variants and any ties at the Kth place This is exactly what we want

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: I will use the FETCH FIRST K ROWS WITH TIES method because it is more straightforward and does not require a window function

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Final query WITH case_sequences AS SELECT case_id list activity ORDER BY timestamp AS activity_sequence FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id variant_counts AS SELECT activity_sequence COUNT AS variant_count FROM case_sequences GROUP BY activity_sequence ORDER BY variant_count DESC FETCH FIRST K ROWS WITH TIES SELECT el FROM event_log el JOIN case_sequences cs ON el case_id equals cs case_id JOIN variant_counts vc ON cs activity_sequence equals vc activity_sequence

Name: Validation - PE
Text: This query will For each case create an ordered list of activities Group by that list to count the number of cases per variant Take the top K variants including ties at the Kth place Return all events for the cases that belong to these top K variants

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Let me test with a sample event_log Example event_log case_id 1 activity A then B case_id 2 activity A then B case_id 3 activity A then C case_id 4 activity A then B For case_id 1 2 and 4 the sequence is A B For case_id 3 it is A C So variant A B has 3 cases A C has 1 case If K equals 1 we want the top 1 variant which is A B Then we return all events for cases 1 2 4

Name: Validation - PE
Text: This matches

Name: Counterfactual Reasoning - IND
Text: If there are multiple activities with the same timestamp for the same case then the order is not specified We are only using timestamp to order If there are ties the order is arbitrary

Name: Validation - PE
Text: But note that the problem says ordered sequence of activities that occur in a single case determined by sorting events within that case by their timestamp So if there are ties the order is not specified We are following the problem
